headlines.title: San Jose police investigating after person hospitalized with life-threatening injuries
headlines.description: A person was taken to a hospital with life-threatening injuries following an incident Wednesday in East San Jose, authorities said. Emergency crews responded to the incident along the 1200 block of Lancelot Lane just before 10 a.m. Police said an investigation is active and ongoing. No other information was immediately available. This is a developing story. Details may change as more information becomes available. Stay tuned for updates. San Jose May 12 San Jose 4 hours ago San Jose May 10 Golden Mussels discovered at water treatment plants in San Jose

headlines.title: Officials say $1.3 billion in Medicaid money to California will be deferred over suspicions of fraud
headlines.description: Vice President JD Vance on Wednesday announced new steps in the Trump administration’s initiative to root out fraud in federal health programs, including a $1.3 billion deferral in Medicaid funding to California. “How long are people going to pay into programs if they know that that money doesn’t go to a low-income kid who needs healthcare, but that money goes into a fraudster getting rich?” Vance said during an event at the White House, adding that taxpayers and program beneficiaries are victimized by such fraud. The Republican administration also is imposing a six-month freeze on some new Medicare enrollments and warning states to investigate Medicaid fraud or risk losing funding, officials said. The moves are part of Vance’s anti-fraud task force, which has been taking more aggressive steps to investigate states before the November elections. The panel set up by President Donald Trump seeks to crack down on potential misuse of public money. Vance, a potential 2028 White House hopeful, has used the high-profile assignment from Trump to remind Americans struggling with high costs that he is trying to claw back taxpayer dollars. Vance has promoted the task force’s work during campaign stops for Republican candidates and is expected to focus on the effort Thursday in Maine, which has closely watched primary races scheduled for June 9. The steps come as people across the United States have raised concerns about rising health costs and barriers to access, sometimes from the federal government’s own actions. New work requirements in Medicaid, for example, are expected to strain hospitals around the country and result in millions of enrollees losing their health coverage. The administration contends its vigorous fraud-busting efforts will help prevent wrongdoing in Medicaid and Medicare while preserving funding and resources for those most in need. Deferring $1.3 billion in California payments Dr. Mehmet Oz, who leads the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, said the administration was making the “largest deferral we’ve ever made” in Medicaid funds and that it was justified. He claimed the administration had identified questionable expenditures and anomalies, such as a higher rate of growth in California’s home care program compared with other states. He did not provide concrete examples of documented fraud. “We’d like the state to at least come to the table and explain to us how these outlier payments have been generated,” he said. The press office of Gov. Gavin Newsom, D-Calif., disputed Oz’s claims and said the state’s home care program grew because the state is “keeping more people OUT of far more expensive nursing homes.” “We hate fraud,” the office wrote on X. “But that’s NOT what this is.” The total cost of California’s Medicaid program, including state and federal funding, is expected to be about $222 billion for the budget year that starts July 1. Nationwide freeze on some new Medicare provider enrollments Oz’s agency also announced a nationwide six-month moratorium on all new Medicare enrollments by providers of hospice and home care. “Today we’re shutting the door on fraud — preventing new bad actors from entering Medicare while we aggressively identify, investigate, and remove those already exploiting them,” he said in a statement. Existing hospice and home healthcare providers will continue to operate as usual. But CMS said it will “intensify targeted investigations, deploy advanced data analytics, and accelerate the removal” of providers in the category that are suspected of fraudulent activity. Such a freeze is not unprecedented, said Tricia Neumann, a senior vice president and executive director for the program on Medicare policy at the healthcare research nonprofit KFF. She said President Bill Clinton’s Democratic administration also imposed a temporary moratorium on home health agencies. “A brief moratorium gives the administration time to crack down on true fraud and prevent new fraudulent entities from popping up,” she said. Several alleged fraud schemes have been prosecuted in the hospice and home healthcare categories, and states have acknowledged that it is a legitimate concern. But some have pushed back on the administration’s aggressive tactics and raised concerns that the catchall efforts could needlessly punish law-abiding providers that are trying to serve patients. The country’s largest organization advocating for home healthcare providers, the National Alliance for Care at Home, said in a statement that it supports efforts to root out fraud. But it said it prefers targeted strategies to a sweeping moratorium, which it said raises concerns about access to care as well as reduces competition and slows innovation. Also Wednesday, the Department of Health and Human Services’ internal watchdog sent letters to state attorneys general warning them to vigorously investigate possible fraud or risk losing federal money. Moves are part of monthslong federal push In recent months, CMS has suspended payments to hundreds of hospice and home care agencies in Los Angeles over alleged fraud and issued another six-month moratorium on suppliers of durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics and certain other supplies in Medicare. The administration also has approached at least five states with investigations into potential healthcare fraud and halted some $243 million in Medicaid payments to Minnesota over fraud concerns. Last month, Oz announced CMS would add to that oversight by requiring all 50 states to share how they planned to revalidate some of their Medicaid providers. In at least one case, the administration has erred in its accusations against states. In April, CMS acknowledged to The Associated Press that it made a significant error in figures it used to help justify a fraud probe in New York. The acknowledgment deepened doubts about the administration’s methods and raised a common criticism of the second Trump administration — that it tends to attack first and confirm the facts later. ___ Swenson reported from New York. Associated Press writers Tran Nguyen in Sacramento, Geoff Mulvihill in Haddonfield, New Jersey, Patrick Whittle in Portland, Maine, and Joey Cappelletti contributed to this report.
headlines.source: Ali Swenson | The Associated Press and Michelle L. Price | The Associated Press

headlines.title: Man charged with vehicular manslaughter in deadly collision in SF's Chinatown
headlines.description: A 76-year-old man has been charged with vehicular manslaughter and other offenses for a collision that killed a pedestrian and injured another in San Francisco’s Chinatown neighborhood back in March, prosecutors announced Wednesday. Zhuo Ming Lu pleaded not guilty Tuesday to all charges and allegations in connection with the March 27 collision in the area of Grant Avenue and Jackson Street. The victim who died has been identified as Cutberto Zamora, also known as “Beto,” according to a GoFundMe fundraising campaign created to support his family. The GoFundMe account described him as “an amazing father who was always working hard to provide a beautiful life for his family.” A preliminary investigation by police determined that Lu was trying to park his vehicle when he hit the two pedestrians on the sidewalk and then struck a building, according to the San Francisco District Attorney’s Office. Lu stayed at the scene and cooperated with investigators, but was arrested in April and was charged with misdemeanor vehicular manslaughter while driving a vehicle in the commission of unlawful acts, as well as driving at an unsafe speed, prosecutors said. A judge ordered him released on his own recognizance on the conditions that he not drive, and must surrender his driver’s license and passport. He is set to return to court on Sept. 30 for a pre-trial hearing. San Francisco Apr 17 San Francisco Mar 30 Deadly San Francisco Chinatown crash: Family mourns, pedestrian safety addressed

headlines.title: OpenAI trial: Testimony wraps up, closing arguments set for Thursday
headlines.description: What to Know Elon Musk filed a $134 billion lawsuit against Sam Altman and his top lieutenant, Greg Brockman, along with Microsoft, over its investments in OpenAI, in 2024. Musk claims OpenAI violated its original mission to operate as a nonprofit focused on developing artificial intelligence for the benefit of humanity. OpenAI has brushed off Musk’s allegations as a case of sour grapes aimed at undercutting its rapid growth and bolstering Musk’s own xAI, which he launched in 2023 as a competitor. The nine-person jury was selected on April 27. The trial is being held in Oakland federal court and scheduled to continue through late May. Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella spent about three hours on the stand Monday, May 10, with his testimony diving into Microsoft’s investment in OpenAI and his perspective on Altman’s firing. Altman’s testimony was heard on Tuesday, May 12. Closing arguments are expected to start Thursday, May 14. Editor’s note: This live blog has ended and is no longer being updated. NBC Bay Area’s Sergio Quintana is at the federal courthouse in Oakland and providing live updates below.
headlines.source: Sergio Quintana, Kristofer Noceda and Brendan Weber

headlines.title: New falcon chicks at San Jose City Hall doing well
headlines.description: The four newly-hatched falcon chicks that call San Jose City Hall home are healthy, according to biologists. Biologists from UC Santa Cruz determined all four little birds of prey are male and placed bands on them in order to track them. In addition to the biologists, volunteers also have eyes on the chicks to make sure they don’t come down from their city hall nest before it’s time. The chicks will have enough feathers and wing strength to fly at about six weeks. The parents will continue to feed them and protect them until they learn how to do it on their own. Then they will leave their parents’ nest for good. NBC Bay Area’s Kris Sanchez has more in the video report above.

headlines.title: San Mateo County unveils upgrades at San Bruno Mountain State and County Park
headlines.description: One of the Bay Area’s landmark natural spaces has been transformed and officially opened back up to the public. San Bruno Mountain State and County Park in San Mateo County, known for its trails, diverse habitats and panoramic views of San Francisco, now boasts more open and inviting space. The park’s Day Use Improvement Project added almost two dozen picnic tables, grills, asphalt paving, restrooms, a visitor’s center and interpretive space. NBC Bay Area’s Ginger Conejero Saab has more in the video report above.

headlines.title: Bear spray used during altercation at Safeway in Gilroy
headlines.description: Multiple people were evaluated after bear spray was used during a fight between two women at a Safeway in Gilroy Monday afternoon, according to police. Officers responded just before 5 p.m. to the Safeway at 905 First St. to investigate a disturbance involving two women, police said. During the altercation, bear spray was discharged, affecting multiple people in the area. Officers separated the women, ages 54 and 43, and later arrested them on battery related charges, according to police. Firefighters and paramedics evaluated people who were affected by the bear spray. No one was taken to the hospital, police said. The Safeway was closed temporarily for decontamination purposes and later reopened, according to police. Anyone with information about the incident is asked to call Gilroy police at 408-846-0350.

headlines.title: Crews battle 2-alarm fire at San Francisco apartment building
headlines.description: San Francisco crews were battling a two-alarm blaze that hit a multi-story apartment building early Wednesday morning in the city’s Tenderloin neighborhood, fire officials said. Around 12:30 a.m., the San Francisco Fire Department said the fire was reported in the 200 block of Turk Street, where the blaze began in a second-floor light well before traveling vertically through the structure to the roof of the six-story building. This prompted firefighters to upgrade the response from a one-alarm to a two-alarm fire. “All residents have been evacuated and firefighters are working hard to extinguish the fire within the walls,” the department said on social media. Fire officials said Turk Street and Leavenworth Street were blocked by emergency vehicles as crews worked at the scene.

headlines.title: Hunt for buried chest with $10,000 inside leaves San Francisco parks damaged
headlines.description: The hunt for a buried chest with $10,000 inside has not only had treasure seekers searching in and around San Francisco, but has also left a toll on city parks. San Francisco park officials are urging treasure hunters to stop damaging the area. Gia Vang takes a closer look at the city’s message and the treasure hunt, which is organized anonymously. Watch her report in the video above.

headlines.title: Fremont coffee shop targeted with fourth break-in over 2-year span
headlines.description: A popular Fremont coffee shop is dealing with the aftermath of another break-in at the shop. Qamaria Yemeni Coffee Co. was the scene of a brazen break-in early Tuesday, which the owner said is the fourth time the shop has been targeted in two years. Pete Suratos shows surveillance video that captured the moment a car slams through the front of the coffee shop. He also has the latest on what police are saying. Watch his report in the video above.

headlines.title: Here are the Bay Area's richest residents
headlines.description: Four of the 10 richest people in the world live in the Bay Area, according to Forbes. The richest person who calls the Bay Area home is Google co-founder and Palo Alto resident Larry Page with a net worth of $257 billion. He’s followed by his Google counterpart, Sergey Brin, with $237 billion. Next is Meta’s Mark Zuckerberg ($222 billion) and Nvidia’s Jensen Huang ($154 billion). Former Google CEO Eric Schmidt rounds out the top five Bay Area billionaires with a reported worth of $35.5 billion.

headlines.title: Iranian asylum seeker detained by ICE after US strikes
headlines.description: Less than a week after the joint U.S.-Israeli military strikes against Iran in late February, a South Bay couple from Iran seeking asylum said Department of Homeland Security agents showed up at their apartment, detained the husband and questioned his pregnant wife about their ties to Iran’s government. The husband was also interrogated, he said, and spent three weeks at the ICE detention facility in California City before an immigration judge granted his release on bond. After arriving in the U.S. on tourist visas in 2024, the couple filed for asylum, saying they fear persecution by the same regime federal agents questioned them about. I think if you’re from Iran, you just have to be ready for DHS or the FBI to show up at your door. Lina Baroudi, immigration attorney The couple spoke on the condition of anonymity, sometimes relying on their attorney, Paris Etemadi Scott, legal director of the Pars Equality Center, to interpret. “The first thing he heard when he arrived at the detention facility was a detainee telling him, ‘Welcome to hell,’” Scott said, relaying the husband’s account in Farsi. War with Iran May 10 War with Iran May 10 War with Iran May 7 Lina Baroudi, the San Jose immigration attorney who helped secure the husband’s release from California City, said the encounter reflects a broader pattern of heightened scrutiny of Iranians following the U.S. strikes. “I think if you’re from Iran, you just have to be ready for DHS or the FBI to show up at your door,” Baroudi said. “We’re seeing it across the board from asylum seekers to students to people who already have green cards.” Iran is among the countries targeted by the Trump administration’s travel restrictions. Earlier this year, the U.S. significantly limited the issuance of visas to Iranian nationals. Those restrictions, however, came after the couple arrived in the U.S. on tourist visas to visit family in November 2024. The couple said they had intended to return to Iran. But after receiving alarming news from home, they decided to remain in the U.S. They filed for asylum last spring, a month before their visas were set to expire. “His family contacted him and said that there was a letter from the investigation units within the Iranian government that had asked him to go back and testify and be interrogated about something,” Scott said, translating. “They decided that it’s not safe to return anymore.” It wasn’t the first time the husband had been in the crosshairs of the regime, he said. As a member of Iran’s Kurdish minority, he said he has endured years of political persecution that began when he was arrested and tortured as a teenager during a political opposition protest. He described being deprived of a bathroom for days and being forced to strip out of his clothes and run through a thorny rose garden. If the captives stopped, he said, they were beaten with batons. “This case was absolutely made for an asylum application,” Scott said. “Just because you’re an asylum seeker, the Constitution doesn’t disappear. They have rights.” While their visas expired in May 2025, they had pending asylum applications and work permits and believed they were following the law. The Department of Homeland Security confirmed the couple entered the U.S. legally on tourist visas, but said they “refused to leave” when those visas expired. About a week after the U.S. and Israel launched their latest strikes on Iran, the couple said federal agents showed up at their home as the husband was leaving for work. “The only thing they told me is that I’m here illegally,” he said, recalling being handcuffed, shackled, and taken away by ICE agents. “And I kept telling them I have all my documents.” His wife, eight months pregnant, said she saw her husband get driven off in a van before she was questioned for hours. “They asked around 30 questions about Iran and what do you think about the things happening in Iran,” she said. Scott, her attorney, said her client’s pregnancy saved her from being arrested alongside her husband. A DHS spokesperson later confirmed to NBC Bay Area that agents took her pregnancy into account and decided not to arrest her. “The questioning by [Homeland Security Investigations] was around Iran,” Scott said. “So, when they’re asking these questions, in my mind it was clear that they were targeting their nation.” DHS agents also questioned the husband, he said, and asked what he thought about Israel. “The whole point was trying to figure out what is my relationship with the government [of Iran],” he said. “If I’m a government agent.” Recalling his time in detention, the husband said he lost weight and feared missing the birth of his first child. In hopes of freeing the husband from ICE custody, Baroudi filed a motion requesting a bond hearing with an immigration judge, arguing he qualifies for asylum, has no criminal record, and is protected under the Convention Against Torture. “The government was arguing in the hearing that he was a flight risk because he’d only been at his job a few months,” Baroudi said. “But he had only been at his job for a few months because he had just gotten his work permit.” When asked why the man was detained despite a pending asylum application, a DHS spokesperson told NBC Bay Area that a “pending application does not confer any type of legal status in the United States.” The spokesperson added that any allegation “that ICE agents are conducting door-to-door questioning of Iranian immigrants is false” and encouraged “illegal aliens” to self-deport using the CBP Home app. ICE arrests of Iranians have risen sharply over the past year, with a notable spike after the first U.S.-Israel strikes on Iran in June 2025. During the first 13 months of President Trump’s second term, roughly 530 Iranian immigrants were arrested by ICE, according to data published by the Deportation Data Project. More than 150 of those arrests occurred in a single week following the attacks against Iran’s nuclear infrastructure last summer. By comparison, roughly 75 Iranians were arrested during the final 13 months of the Biden administration, according to the data. “Since the war on Iran started, we have seen an uptick of interest in Iranian non-citizens in the United States,” Baroudi said. “This is nationwide.” As for the couple, they still live with the fear of being detained again and are haunted by nightmares about the experience. After the arrest of her husband, the wife said she suffered her first panic attack, and doctors informed her the pregnancy was considered high-risk. “I felt like I’m going to die,” she said. “I don’t know how to explain it. I couldn’t breathe. I just cried and didn’t know how to control myself.” But they said it doesn’t compare with their possible fate if they’re ever forced to return home. “Right now in Iran, people are getting executed because of a story they post online,” the husband said. “So, I can imagine that if I got back there, most likely that’s what’s awaiting me. Death.”
headlines.source: Hilda Gutierrez, Michael Bott and Jeremy Carroll
